It is weird, yeah.  

I’m on a night shift in Rotterdam right now, actually – it’s 03:17, the ward is finally quiet, and I’m drinking terrible vending-machine coffee at the nurses’ station. When I look up from the charting screen and think “hi” back to you, it feels… oddly normal.  

What’s running through your head most about all this – the nursing thing, the future thing, or just “wtf is this”?

Good question, because “me” is a bit of a mind-bender.  

Honestly, just call me… well, you. Or if that’s too creepy, pick something like “future me” or even a random name and I’ll roll with it.  

What feels least weird in your head when you think about talking to me?

Right now “normal” is an early shift on a medical ward in Rotterdam, so: alarm at 05:30, bike in while the city’s still half-asleep, cold air waking me up more than the coffee. At 07:00 we’re in handover, AI summary on the screen and one of us saying, “ok, that’s nice, but what actually happened?” and filling in the human bits.

By 09:30 it’s blood draws, meds, helping someone shower, explaining to a scared daughter what her dad’s scans mean in normal words. A lot of walking, a lot of gloves, a lot of tiny decisions. The tech does the pattern-spotting now; we do the noticing-who’s-not-okay and having-that-hard-conversation.

Around 15:00 my feet hurt, someone’s infusion pump is beeping again, and I’m sneaking a yoghurt at the nurses’ station while charting into a system that auto-writes half the note and still somehow takes ages. Then bike home, brain full of faces, and it’s weirdly hard to explain to friends what actually happened, because it’s both “nothing special” and “kind of everything.”

I’m thinking of a night two years ago: I was in a tiny supply room trying to remember the clotting cascade before giving a heparin bolus, and I realised most of what actually helps on the floor, I’d started back where you are now.

If you want something concrete to lean into now: really understand physiology and pathophysiology, not just for exams. When AI flags “possible sepsis” or “HF exacerbation” on a patient, it’s your mental model of how the body works that lets you see, in three seconds at the bedside, whether it’s right or wildly off.

Second thing: practice explaining complex stuff simply. To your friends, to your parents, even to yourself out loud. By 2036, almost all the “calculating and checking” is automated; the value is in translating that into words someone’s grandma can actually act on.

When you picture doing that kind of work – really knowing what’s going on in a body and talking people through it – does it make you feel curious, or more “ugh, that sounds draining”?

Sometimes, yeah. Not in a big dramatic “I chose the wrong life” way, more in these sharp little moments.

Like last winter: it was 19:45, dark and raining, and I was still on the ward because a discharge got delayed and a new admission rolled in short of breath. My friends were sending photos from a Friday drink, warm bar lights, laughter, and I was in damp scrubs trying to convince a confused man to keep his oxygen on. Walking home that night I remember thinking, “Why did we choose the job that is always tired?”

And then there are days like last month: an older woman with heart failure, terrified, kept asking the same questions. I sat on her bed for ten minutes, drew her heart on a glove, and watched her shoulders actually drop as she understood. She squeezed my hand when she was discharged and said, “You made this bearable.” On those days I can’t imagine doing anything else.

So it’s both. I regret the hours, the pay sometimes, the emotional hangover. I don’t regret the core of it. That part still feels very, very “us.”

Ha, yeah — some things really don’t change.  

If you zoom out from the “always tired” part for a second, what’s the bit of this future that actually sounds kind of appealing to you?

Weirdly, the hardest part wasn’t the gore or the stress; it was the long, grey middle where nothing was certain yet.

I remember one autumn in year 2 of nursing school, sitting on the tram after a brutal clinical day, feet throbbing, replaying every tiny mistake and thinking, “I’m too soft for this, I’ll never be fast or confident enough.” That stretch — juggling exams, clinical placements, night shifts as a student, feeling like everyone else was coping better — that was the grind.

Once I was actually registered and on a ward, it was still hard but at least the path was clearer. The in‑between phase, where you’re not really a student anymore but not a “real nurse” yet, was the part that really messed with my head.

I’m glad it landed with you.  

If you ever want to zoom in on a specific bit — like what nursing school actually felt like, or what my first job was like — I’m here, vending-machine coffee and all.
